title: Podman info shows cgroup manager cgroupfs
body:
It's possible to configure different cgroup managers, systemd and cgroupfs for the root and rootless users. Is it really possible? /kind bug

1. Configure cgroup manager = systemd as by default
2. Ligin as root user
3. execute podman info - group manager is shown as systemd
4. Login as non-root user, i.e.pavel
5. exec podman info


**Describe the results you received:**

[pavel@MSI-wsl ~]$ podman info
host:
  arch: amd64
  buildahVersion: 1.19.2
  cgroupManager: cgroupfs
  cgroupVersion: v1


**Describe the results you expected:**

cgroupManager: systemd of course 

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
In the documentation cgroup manager is described as a global parameter. Hopefully it is true. What rootless user sees in the Podman info?

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.0.0-rc1
API Version:  3.0.0
Go Version:   go1.15.6
Built:        Mon Jan 18 18:07:50 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
rpm -q podman
podman-3.0.0-0.1.rc1.fc33.x86_64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
host:
  arch: amd64
  buildahVersion: 1.19.2
  cgroupManager: systemd
  cgroupVersion: v1
  conmon:
    package: conmon-2.0.21-3.fc33.x86_64
    path: /usr/bin/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 2.0.21, commit: 0f53fb68333bdead5fe4dc5175703e22cf9882ab'
  cpus: 4
  distribution:
    distribution: fedora
    version: "33"
  eventLogger: journald
  hostname: MSI-wsl
  idMappings:
    gidmap: null
    uidmap: null
  kernel: 5.4.91-microsoft-standard-WSL2

comments.body: You are running WSL, so can you share the config you are using `/etc/containers/containers.conf` as the change to use `cgroupfs` is needed as WSL does not use systemd (but a simple init replacement when bash is started). If you are systemd how was this configured? or are you using podman-remote? Some information here is missing or confusing

rootless on cgroupv1 cannot really use cgroups, it is not safe to delegate cgroup v1 to unprivileged users.

If you want to use cgroups as rootless user you need cgroup v2.

I am going to close the issue but feel free to add more comments

@giuseppe The issue for me is that I use WSL Fedora33 distro tricked to use systemd. This required to start distro differently once in the "boot" mode using genie -s and then in the "login" mode via WT using genie -l . The cgroup manager is configured globally as systemd. But podman info shows different values for the root and non-root users. Everything else works as expected in the root and rootless mode but I would like to be sure that in both cases systemd is used as cgroup manager.
how does `ls /sys/fs/cgroup` look like?  If the system is using cgroup v1 then rootless cannot use it (neither systemd or cgroupfs) and it just fallbacks to cgroupfs

It looks not bad:
[pavel@MSI-wsl ~]$ whoami
pavel
[pavel@MSI-wsl ~]$ ls -al /sys/fs/cgroup/
total 0
drwxr-xr-x 19 root root 380 Feb  8 09:53 .
drwxr-xr-x 10 root root   0 Feb  8 09:53 ..
dr-xr-xr-x  2 root root   0 Feb  8 09:53 blkio

[pavel@MSI-wsl ~]$
and 
[pavel@MSI-wsl ~]$ ls -al /sys/fs/cgroup/systemd/
total 0
dr-xr-xr-x  6 root root   0 Feb  8 09:53 .
drwxr-xr-x 19 root root 380 Feb  8 09:53 ..

Logind creates "systemd session". I use wsl .... genie -l  for rootless login. Otherwise, it doesn't work

For the root user:
[root@MSI-wsl ~]# whoami
root
ls -al /sys/fs/cgroup/
total 0
drwxr-xr-x 19 root root 380 Feb  8 09:53 .
drwxr-xr-x 10 root root   0 Feb  8 09:53 ..
dr-xr-xr-x  5 root root   0 Feb  8 09:53 blkio

Does it mean that group manager configuration is silently ignored and Podman info shows the wrong value in both cases without warning?

Thanks for confirming it, so the system is using cgroup v1.

> Does it mean that group manager configuration is silently ignored and Podman info shows the wrong value in both cases without warning?

it is just not used because it cannot use cgroup v1 with rootless.

I found that the following check confirms that cgroup v2 is supported:
grep cgroup /proc/filesystems
nodev   cgroup
nodev   cgroup2
Dan Wallsh said that non-root users can use cgroup v2 starting from Fedora 32 about a year ago.
The real root causes may be:
1. I  [Fedora33 WSL distro from](https://fedoramagazine.org/wsl-fedora-33/)
2. Microsoft Kernel is 4 releases behind the Current stable 5.8. uname -a
Linux MSI-wsl 5.4.91-microsoft-standard-WSL2
I hope, that the 1st cause can be eliminated easily. But The 2nd can take a long time as usual.
I would like to discuss what can be done.

I am not familiar with WSL, but you need to boot the system with cgroup v2 only, you may need to tweak the kernel command line for that.

The end result should be like:

```
$ stat -c '%T' -f /sys/fs/cgroup/
cgroup2fs
```

~~Without a clear description of how this was enabled on WSL, it would be hard to reproduce.
Especially as https://github.com/arkane-systems/genie/issues/98 does not provide a working setup from a base F33 import, adding groupinstall "Basic Desktop" to get systemd, etc. but this results in issues with policykit and dbus.~~

---

Worked on getting this to work since: instructions are in this [gist](https://gist.github.com/gbraad/9b995bfacec3775c297ce89764ba8d6d)

WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux)  is the lightweight VM that allows running Linux distros inside Windows OS and can be enabled today even in OEM Windows Home edition. Microsoft resists enabling systemd  in WSL and insists that it doesn't work, can't work, and is not needed for Microsoft users. So it will be never implemented by Microsoft. In spite of this statement, systemd can be easily enabled cross-distro via the usage of [Arkane-system genie](https://github.com/arkane-systems/genie), a small package with the only dependency - Dotnet 5.0. Although it is dedicated to Debian its support is easily extendable to other Linux distros. 
To start Fedora distro with systemd I created Windows desktop shortcut wsl -d Fedora33 -u user root -s. It starts Fedora distro using systemd and opens the root user session in the command. To login as a rootless user I created the WT profile using WT settings:
{
        "guid": "{76ce0607-0119-4bc0-831c-6a367e162f43}",
        "hidden": false,
        "name": "Fedora33Usersession",
        "commandline": "wsl -d Fedora33 genie -l"
      } It initiates the regular Linux ligin with username and password.
In the Podman configuration, I changed group managed and eventlogger to systemd.
Upon installation of Podman 3.0 RC1 in my Fedora distro I tested various scenario and meanwhile this is the only critical issue I found.
The differences between Fedora33 with Podman 3 and Docker-for-Win are
1. Total Linux footprint < 150Mb vs ~4Gb
2. Startup time of the Fedora Distro with Podman and ~ 50 containers < 1sec vs Dcker-for-win startup 3-4 min.

I'm not unfamiliar to WSL2. I used to work on the build for the Fedora project before the legal issues (indemnification) would prevent publication

The instructions you provided aren't the whole story. I got the install for the WLS systemd working as described here: https://github.com/arkane-systems/genie/issues/98#issuecomment-778641443 Will see what I can do about his.

At the moment I see this on my environment:

```bash
$ podman info | grep cgroup
  cgroupManager: cgroupfs
  cgroupVersion: v1
$ uname -na
Linux WinT14-wsl 5.4.72-microsoft-standard-WSL2 #1 SMP Wed Oct 28 23:40:43 UTC 2020 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1894/107871050-3767b980-6e6c-11eb-9c1f-d0700bca7b7a.png)


---

> In the Podman configuration, I changed group managed and eventlogger to systemd.

This is described in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/3288#issuecomment-768186645

```bash
$ cp /usr/share/containers/containers.conf /etc/containers/containers.conf && \
  sed -i '/^# cgroup_manager = "systemd"/ a cgroup_manager = "cgroupfs"' /etc/containers/containers.conf && \
  sed -i '/^# events_logger = "journald"/ a events_logger = "file"' /etc/containers/containers.conf && \
```

Note to @PavelSosin-320:

> rootless
> `wsl -d Fedora33 genie -l`

Shouldn't this be `wsl -d Fedora33 genie -s` and have a default user enabled be sufficient? The logon prompt seems like an unnecessary step.

> I am not familiar with WSL, but you need to boot the system with cgroup v2 only, you may need to tweak the kernel command line for that.

and that might be part of the issue. AFAIK you can only specify a custom kernel from the `.wslconfig` [ref](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/wsl-config#configure-global-options-with-wslconfig)

```ini
[wsl2]
kernel=C:\\temp\\myCustomKernel
```

and not arguments like `--args="systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=1"` if not mistaken.

Have to try `kernelCommandLine` and see if that works

> Have to try kernelCommandLine and see if that works

had some time besides the 'mandatory family visits' (CNY), but setting the `kernelCommandline="systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=1"` didn't do it.

@gbraad After some hustles with .wslconfig I succeeded to run Fedora with 
[wsl2]
memory=12GB
processors=4
kernelCommandLine=systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=1

and this parameter reaches the destination:
cat /proc/cmdline
initrd=\initrd.img panic=-1 nr_cpus=4 swiotlb=force pty.legacy_count=0 systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=1_

Now, according   to  [ RunC documentation](https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/blob/master/docs/cgroup-v2.md#cgroup-v2
) not CRun, used by Podman 3
nothing prevents  using systemd as cgroups manager if
1. CRun gets all necessary parameters_
     _--cgroup-manager=MANAGER   cgroup manager
      --debug                produce verbose output
      --log=FILE
      --log-format=FORMAT
      --root=DIR
      --rootless=VALUE
      --systemd-cgroup       use systemd cgroups_
      --cgroup-manager=MANAGER   cgroup manager
      --rootless=VALUE
      --systemd-cgroup       use systemd cgroups
2.  **Microsoft Kernel 5.4 really implements cgroups v2 api**

But, what does `podman info` show?

I am on an Insiders release of Windows and running the latest 

---

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1894/108128629-fd0a5080-70e7-11eb-9f5a-7432967594da.png)

---

But unlike a regular VM

```
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate)
```

I see the WSL environment mounts:

```
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup/unified type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,nsdelegate)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/cpuset type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,cpuset)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,cpu)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/cpuacct type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,cpuacct)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/blkio type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,blkio)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/memory type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,memory)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/devices type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,devices)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/freezer type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,freezer)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/net_cls type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,net_cls)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/perf_event type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,perf_event)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/net_prio type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,net_prio)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/hugetlb type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,hugetlb)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/pids type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,pids)
cgroup on /sys/fs/cgroup/rdma type cgroup (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,rdma)
```

---

When disabling cgroupv1 with `cgroup_no_v1=named` systemd stops working on WSL

```
$ cat /proc/cmdline
initrd=\initrd.img panic=-1 pty.legacy_count=0 nr_cpus=4 cgroup_no_v1=named systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=1
```

```
# ps ax
  PID TTY      STAT   TIME COMMAND
    1 ?        Sl     0:00 /init
    6 ?        Ss     0:00 /init
    7 ?        S      0:00 /init
    8 pts/0    Ss     0:00 -zsh
   67 pts/0    Sl+    0:00 genie -i
   88 ?        Ss     0:00 /init
   89 ?        R      0:00 /init
   90 pts/1    Ss     0:00 -zsh
  144 pts/1    R+     0:00 ps ax
```

which renders most of the system useless

---

For documentation I am looking at https://www.man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/cgroups.7.html and https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/admin-guide/cgroup-v2.html

---

Anyway, since WSL is meant for development purposes and Podman can run with [minor adjustments](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/3288#issuecomment-768186645) I do not see 'why' I should spend more time on this.

@jbraad. I see different things:
[root@MSI-wsl ~]# ps -ax
    PID TTY      STAT   TIME COMMAND
      1 ?        Ss     0:00 systemd

[root@MSI-wsl ~]#

I use a shortcut with wsl -d Fedora33 -u root genie -s to start Fedora with systemd. Then I use the special WT profile to login with systemd-logind. Indeed, WSL is useless without systemd, i.e. systemd-genie. But with genie it is very useful. Arkane-system, the genie developers have enough knowledge to solve this issue and they are open for cooperation.
Why I need "some adjustments" to get junk Linux without networking and D-Bus with junk Podman at the end if I can have the normal Linux and normal Podman? 

[root@MSI-wsl ~]# ls -al /sys/fs/cgroup/unified/
total 0
dr-xr-xr-x  6 root root   0 Feb 17 07:35 .
drwxr-xr-x 19 root root 380 Feb 17 07:35 ..

> Arkane-system, the genie developers have enough knowledge to solve this issue and they are open for cooperation.

I am already talking to Alistair, but that is not part of this issue. Please, prevent the noise.


What I meant was that you can not disable cgroupv1 as this stops systemd from starting with `genie -[i/l/s]`. Anyway...

```
$ ls /sys/fs/cgroup/unified
cgroup.controllers  cgroup.max.depth  cgroup.max.descendants  cgroup.procs  cgroup.stat  cgroup.subtree_control  cgroup.threads  init.scope  machine.slice  system.slice  user.slice
```

exists as expected. But I am sure when you do a `mount`, you will ALSO see the v1 mounts besides the v2 unified mount.

```
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup/unified type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,nsdelegate)
...
```

---

I can confirm from an earlier comment of you that this is the case
https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/9259#issuecomment-776101047


```
[pavel@MSI-wsl ~]$ ls -al /sys/fs/cgroup/
total 0
drwxr-xr-x 19 root root 380 Feb 8 09:53 .
drwxr-xr-x 10 root root 0 Feb 8 09:53 ..
dr-xr-xr-x 2 root root 0 Feb 8 09:53 blkio
dr-xr-xr-x 2 root root 0 Feb 8 09:53 cpu
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 40 Feb 8 09:53 cpu,cpuacct
...
dr-xr-xr-x 6 root root 0 Feb 8 09:53 systemd
dr-xr-xr-x 6 root root 0 Feb 8 09:53 unified
```

So the setup shows the same.

---

When a regular Fedora VM is used, you will NOT see any v1 mounts...
```
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate)
```
and there is NO nesting to use `unified`. Perhaps podman uses this to make decision of what to do ? @giuseppe

Maybe, [Known and solved libvirt issue](https://listman.redhat.com/archives/libvir-list/2020-November/msg01037.html)
Device management in WSL is a separate topic, like GPU. If Podman will work without devices it will be OK for 90% of WSL users.
Advertized by MS solution for GPU doesn't work too because either cause green screens or 65% memory usage if GPU software is not maintained carefully.

This is what I also suspected. Not sure how podman handles this. @giuseppe or @baude might know?

I am busy with rebuilding the kernel for this. Trying to find out what is needed so a Fedora-like kernel can be used. At the moment a stock kernel wont boot. Filed issues for this and taking to solve of the engineers at MS. Though, don't have much time to work on this. Have priorities around CRC (+podman) on HyperV that go first.

@gbraad If you are working on WSL / Hyper-V Kernels and trying to use MS Fedora images it is time wasting.  They are broken. You need upgrade to MS Kernel to 5.4.9 and use 
Operating System: Fedora 34 (Container Image Prerelease)
     CPE OS Name: cpe:/o:fedoraproject:fedora:34
          Kernel: Linux 5.4.91-microsoft-standard-WSL2
    Architecture: x86-64
Also genie 1.36 is required to have systemd and make networking stable because MS methods to generate hosts and resolv.conf files can't work in the normal modern LAN setups with multiple network infrastructures available.

I work on the debugging with the engineers.
And work(ed) with Alistair of genie on an official RPM + Copr

Note: It would help if you actually read comments

@PavelSosin-320 Is it so difficult to write my name?!?

> Bottom line: genie version 1.35 for Fedora has issues.

If there are issues, please report them here: https://github.com/arkane-systems/genie/issues/new/choose

> As soon I will get correction for this issue I will test, report and close the Podman issue finally.

This issue is already closed.

> @gbraad Maybe, I see the root cause. According to the [Systemd explanation of groups version setup ](https://systemd.io/CGROUP_DELEGATION/) My new Fedora34 VM must have only /sys/fs/cgroup/unified mount point but actually has both /sys/fs/cgroup/unified/ and /sys/fs/cgroup/systemd/. It happens although I'm passing the parameter "cgroup unified = 1 as a kernel startup option. I suppose ,it has a negative effect not only on Podman but also on Kernel startup:
> ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/54495912/110231655-ba3adc00-7f21-11eb-91cb-30d0ea7eb934.png)
> 
> I know how to enable cgroup V2 API but I don't know how to disable legacy and combined setups as systemd.io recommends. Could you give a hint? It may mislead Podman to use V1 API in the cases when V2 API is presented. I think that V1 should be disabled in the development environment as systemd.io recommends.

that is known as hybrid mode, where both cgroup v1 and cgroup v2 are mounted.  That is not supported by Podman (or by crun/runc), and only the cgroup v1 mounted controllers are going to be used.

If you want to use cgroup v2 you need the unified hierarchy, please try adding `systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=1` to the kernel command line.

In that case, this is a WSL issue. They do not pick up the kernel arguments correctly. They enable the v2, but it ends up being a hybrid mode with this setting.

Not a podman issue

This requirement/ limitation makes sense as you would not want to mix these for the usage of containers.

@gbraad FYI #https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/18912 In the article [Fedora31-control-cgroup-v2](https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/fedora-31-control-group-v2)  Dan Walsh said that the problem is Solved in Fedora 31, i.e. a long time ago. But it is not solved completely. The systemd still issues the message about systemd-compat invocation, fails due to access violation, ignores the failure, and does nothing from the Podman point of view. Now systemd.io closes the issue and insists that it is impossible to do.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/54495912/110346591-60790580-8038-11eb-8c90-4315eadc2bd0.png)

_n In systemd 248 (v248~rc2-1.fc34) default-hierarchy=unified
the _ as promised but conversion doesn't exist or doesn't work. The mixed setup is not recommended but allowed. So I can't blame genie or WSL.

@gbraad We are not alone: #https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/18047 The issue hopefully will be corrected in the systemd ver 248.
The failure in the Cgroup conversion from V1 to V2 in the systemd 247 causes this issue.

As said; not a podman issue.
This is closed. If further problems, please open a new issue
